edges.description: Inappropriately low hepcidin releases the ferroportin brake on intestinal iron efflux.

edges.description: BMP6-linked hepcidin dysregulation converges on insufficient hepcidin restraint of ferroportin.

edges.description: Non-HFE hepcidin-pathway defects converge on inadequate hepcidin restraint of ferroportin-mediated iron export.

edges.description: Increased intestinal ferroportin-mediated export expands the plasma iron pool.

edges.description: Plasma iron-pool expansion raises transferrin saturation, the diagnostic hallmark of hemochromatosis.
Progressive systemic iron overload increases ferritin as a circulating marker of iron stores.
Increased gut iron transfer elevates circulating iron and drives hepatic deposition.
Chronic plasma iron loading promotes myocardial iron accumulation.
Systemic iron excess leads to iron deposition in pancreatic islets.
Systemic iron loading affects endocrine tissues, especially pituitary gonadotropes.
Toxic iron species and joint deposition contribute to hemochromatosis arthropathy.
Iron loading contributes to bronze skin pigmentation.
Iron deposition in bone and gonadal injury contribute to low bone density and fracture risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Iron deposition in bone and gonadal endocrine injury.

edges.description: Multi-organ iron overload produces constitutional symptoms including fatigue and weight loss.
intermediate_mechanisms: Multi-organ iron deposition, chronic inflammatory stress, and endocrine/liver complications.

edges.description: Hepatocyte injury and remodeling contribute to progressive liver enlargement.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocyte injury, liver remodeling, and organ enlargement.

edges.description: Hepatocellular injury releases circulating hepatic transaminases.
Hepatic iron loading and liver enlargement can manifest as abdominal pain.
intermediate_mechanisms: Liver enlargement, capsular distension, and hepatic inflammation.

edges.description: Chronic iron-mediated hepatocellular injury and fibrotic remodeling can progress to cirrhosis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Iron-mediated oxidative injury, fibrosis, and architectural remodeling.

edges.description: Advanced iron-mediated liver injury and carcinogenic iron stress increase HCC risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Iron-mediated oxidative DNA damage, fibrosis, and cirrhosis.

edges.description: Oxidative myocardial injury and contractile dysfunction manifest as cardiomyopathy.
Beta-cell dysfunction from iron toxicity impairs insulin output and drives diabetes.
intermediate_mechanisms: Beta-cell oxidative injury, impaired insulin secretion, and sustained hyperglycemia.

edges.description: Impaired beta-cell insulin secretion first manifests as elevated blood glucose.
intermediate_mechanisms: Beta-cell oxidative injury and impaired insulin secretion.

edges.description: Pituitary iron deposition disrupts gonadotropin output.
Hypogonadism from pituitary iron toxicity can impair male sexual function.
Gonadotropin deficiency from pituitary iron toxicity can suppress menstrual cycling.
Pituitary siderosis can also cause secondary hypothyroidism in severe hemochromatosis.
Joint iron-associated tissue injury manifests as hemochromatosis arthropathy.
Dermal iron and pigment deposition produce bronze hyperpigmentation.
Iron-associated bone remodeling abnormalities manifest as osteoporosis.
Hypogonadism and systemic iron-overload effects contribute to reduced muscle mass.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hypogonadism, chronic systemic illness, and reduced musculoskeletal reserve.

edges.description: Progressive systemic iron overload manifests as chronic fatigue.
Advanced systemic iron overload can produce unintentional weight loss.
Elevated transferrin saturation reports hepcidin-insufficient iron hyperabsorption and expansion of the plasma iron pool.
Elevated ferritin reflects increased iron stores accompanying systemic iron overload.
Very high ferritin marks increased risk of cirrhosis and mortality in C282Y homozygotes.

evidence.reference_title: The treatment of secondary hemochromatosis.
